ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226934003 represents raw beef steak broadly; no chuck-specific SNOMED CT code was identified. Closest parent match used as the most defensible available code.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw serving of beef chuck steak is approximately 150g, equivalent to one medium-sized steak portion before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, chuck, arm pot roast, separable lean and fat, raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for raw beef chuck. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 13340, Beef, chuck, arm pot roast, separable lean and fat, raw) used as cross-reference for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and extended micronutrient values not reported in HPB.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beef chuck steak, raw contains 0.0g carbohydrate per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. GI is not tested or reported for pure protein/fat foods. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. Numeric value 0 used as sentinel per validator requirement that glycemic_index be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable to indicate GI testing is not relevant for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (58mg/100g) and high potassium (336mg/100g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Beef chuck steak, raw has a very low sodium content of 58mg/100g and a high potassium content of 336mg/100g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio that supports blood pressure management; however, patients should avoid high-sodium marinades, sauces, or brines during preparation and trim visible external fat to reduce saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; high protein content supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef chuck steak, raw contains 0.0g carbohydrate and 0.0g sugar per 100g, producing no glycaemic load; its high protein content of 21.7g/100g slows gastric emptying and supports postprandial blood glucose stability, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion is limited to 90–120g cooked and prepared without high-sugar marinades or glazes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (4.1g/100g) and natural ruminant trans fat (~0.3g/100g) with moderate cholesterol (66mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef chuck steak, raw contains 4.1g saturated fat and approximately 0.3g natural ruminant trans fat (vaccenic and conjugated linoleic acid) per 100g, alongside 66mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to no more than 1–2 servings per week, trim visible fat before cooking, and favour lean preparation methods such as grilling or braising without added saturated fats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid (omega-6) content and saturated fat promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef chuck steak is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 precursor), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-alpha and IL-6 relevant to rheumatoid arthritis disease activity; patients with RA should limit red meat consumption to no more than 1–2 times per week and consider replacing with oily fish such as salmon or mackerel to obtain anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef chuck steak, raw, is an uncooked cut of red meat sourced from the shoulder chuck primal section of beef cattle, typically sold boneless. It displays a deep red colour characteristic of well-oxygenated myoglobin-rich muscle tissue, with rich intramuscular marbling and both internal and external fat attached. The chuck is a heavily worked muscle group, resulting in a flavourful but moderately tough texture that benefits from slow moist-heat cooking methods such as braising, pot-roasting, or slow-cooking, which break down collagen into gelatin and tenderise the meat. When grilled or pan-seared at high heat, it can be served as a steak but requires careful resting. Nutritionally, per 100g raw weight it provides 180 kcal, 21.7g protein, 10.4g total fat (including 4.1g saturated fat and approximately 0.3g natural ruminant trans fat), 0.0g carbohydrate, 58mg sodium, 336mg potassium, 1.74mg iron, and 66mg cholesterol. It is an excellent source of complete protein, zinc (4.8mg), vitamin B12 (2.1mcg), and haem iron, making it nutritionally dense for muscle maintenance and red blood cell production. The zero carbohydrate content means it has no glycaemic impact, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns when portion-controlled. Its moderate saturated fat content warrants attention for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. Halal certification depends on slaughter method. Commonly used in Western cuisines for stews, pot roasts, and grilled steaks.
common_user_queries: How many calories in raw beef chuck steak?
Is beef chuck steak good for diabetes?
Is beef chuck steak high in protein?
Can I eat beef chuck steak if I have high blood pressure?
How much iron is in beef chuck steak?
What is the fat content of beef chuck steak?
Is beef chuck steak suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (confidence 0.95 per input record). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and extended micronutrient values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw beef chuck (NDB 13340), which is an approved secondary source. All values are internally consistent and plausible for raw beef chuck.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as beef chuck steak is a pure animal muscle product containing no plant-derived carbohydrates; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database and USDA FoodData Central. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre. Saturated fat (4.1g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.5g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.4g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.3g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13340 (Beef, chuck, arm pot roast, separable lean and fat, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions separately; these are cross-reference estimates and should be updated if HPB publishes fatty acid breakdown. Trans fat of 0.3g/100g represents natural ruminant trans fat (primarily vaccenic acid and conjugated linoleic acid), not industrially produced trans fat. Vitamin and mineral micronutrient values (vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as HPB does not report these fields for this entry. Vitamin C is 0.0mg as raw beef contains negligible vitamin C. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0g/100g); gi_category is set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol. Numeric value 0 is used for glycemic_index field to satisfy validator numeric-type requirement; this does not imply a measured GI of zero but rather that GI testing is not applicable for this food. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311349 is the best available match for beef chuck steak; FoodEx2 code A0CKL represents beef chuck raw in the EFSA FoodEx2 system. SNOMED CT 226934003 is used as closest_parent match (raw beef steak) as no chuck-specific SNOMED code was identified; gap note documented. Occasion tag 'breakfast' from input cultural data has been retained in meal_context as 'main course' since raw beef chuck is not a typical breakfast item; the original tag may reflect a data entry artefact. Halal status is flagged true per input dietary_tags; kosher status is not confirmed and set to false.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Beef chuck steak, raw (F06030007) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. Two issues from the previous REJECT were resolved: (1) snomed_ct_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent', which is within the allowed set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; (2) glycemic_index changed from null to numeric 0 to satisfy the validator's numeric-type requirement, with gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and notes clarifying that 0 is a sentinel value for a food with negligible carbohydrates. Core macros are complete and sourced from HPB Singapore with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-reference for fatty acid fractions and micronutrients. Zero carbohydrate, sugar, and fibre values are justified as naturally zero for animal muscle tissue. Ontology anchors are defensible and honest. All three mandatory health conditions are present with specific clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Alias consistency verified between flat aliases and aliases_by_language. Western cuisine classification does not trigger mandatory non-English language requirements under G6.
